titleEn: I Revealed a Longing I Had Hidden

verses.sadr: إن كان وجهك لي تترى محاسنه

verses.ajuz: حتى إذا كملت تاهت على التيه

verses.sadr: ما استجمعت فرق الحسن التي افترقت
verses.ajuz: عن يوسف الحسن حتى استجمعت فيه

translationEn: I have spread before you a longing I used to fold away, and I have revealed my anguish that I used to conceal. If your face shows me its beauties one after another, your treatment of me shows me its faults one after another. His lower part sways as he glides along, his upper part trembles as he bends. His form surpassed the forms of all things, and when it reached perfection it surpassed even perfection itself. The scattered parts of beauty that had been dispersed since the time of Joseph the Beautiful were not gathered together until they gathered in him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I fold it up, conceal it
vanity, self-conceit; also the desert of wandering

context: A short ghazal in which Abu Tammam confesses a passion he had long suppressed. The final verse draws a celebrated comparison to the Quranic figure of Joseph (Yusuf), suggesting the beloved has gathered all beauty that was once scattered.

titleAr: لو كنت عندي أمس وهو معانقي
titleEn: Had You Been with Me Yesterday

verses.sadr: لو كنت عندي أمس وهو معانقي

translationEn: Had you been with me yesterday while he was embracing me, my tears flowing down his cheeks — his cheeks were quenched by my tears, and my lips took pleasure in his lips — you would have seen a weeping that love makes easy, and that the releasing of tears over him makes light. And you would have seen something more beautiful than my weeping: his words, 'This young man — his eyes are the ones being stubborn!'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: embracing me, my companion in embrace

context: A tender ghazal imagining an intimate scene of embrace and tears. The final verse turns on a witty reversal: the beloved playfully accuses the poet's eyes of being the obstinate ones, not his heart.

titleEn: His Beauty Surpassed All Description
translationEn: His beauties have excelled so greatly that words are too inadequate to describe him. Beauty itself spoke in defense of his lover before the female censurers, and silenced their preaching. Souls have obtained from him nothing more than what the glance has gained from his cheeks. What harm would it do to one whose beauties are perfect if his harsh heart were to become tender?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the side glance, a brief look

context: A compact ghazal in which Abu Tammam uses beauty itself as an advocate: the beloved's perfection silences all censure. The final verse is a classic reproach — the beloved's cruelty is his only flaw.

titleEn: My Opinion of Him Is Good Despite His Wrong

verses.sadr: لم يلهني عنه ما ألهاه بل عذبت

translationEn: My opinion of him is good, were it not for his wrongdoing and his failure to honor the right of his intimate friend. Nothing has distracted me from him as it has distracted him from me; rather, yearning has been sweet to me as I have drunk of it for his sake. His beauties have pardoned his ill treatment of me, so much so that even his faults have become beautiful to me. This is your lover — longing has made his soul bleed; how then can you deny that his eyelids should bleed?
context: A short ghazal of complaint against an indifferent beloved. The final verse is a powerful plea: if longing has made the lover bleed internally, surely the beloved cannot deny the visible tears as blood.

titleEn: Trials of Time and the Poet's Perseverance

verses.ajuz: يا هذه اعذري في هذه النكب

verses.sadr: في صدره من هموم يعتلجن به

verses.ajuz: فذاب هما وجمد العين لم يذب
ما الدهر في فعله إلا أبو العجب

verses.sadr: فما عدمت بها لا جاحدا عدما
ما يحسم العقل والدنيا تساس يه

verses.ajuz: والعقل عار إذا لم يكس بالنشب

verses.sadr: ما أضيع العقل إن لم يرع ضيعته
verses.ajuz: وفر واي رحى دارت بلا قطب

verses.sadr: كم ذقت في الدهر من عسر ومن يسر
verses.ajuz: وفي بني الدهر من رأس ومن ذنب

verses.sadr: أغضي إذا صرفه لم تغض أعينه
verses.ajuz: عني وأرضى إذا ما لج في الغضب
علما بأني ما قصرت في الطلب
أدراك رزق إذاما كان في الهرب

verses.sadr: ماذا علي إذا لم يزل وتري
verses.ajuz: في الرمي ان زلن أغراضي فلم أصب
تستنبط الصفر لي من معدن الذهب
عن ليلة القدر في شعبان أو رجب
على قواصيه في بدء وفي عقب
لم يخلق العرض مني سوء مطلبي

verses.sadr: إذا عنيت لشأو قلت إني قد
ما آب من آب لم يظفر ببغيته
verses.ajuz: ولم يغب طالب للنجح لم يخب !

translationEn: She came with troubles, and my chief concern turned away from her hints — O you, excuse me in these calamities. Away with you! Woe upon whoever was filled with woe upon you and compassion without cessation — in his breast, griefs that struggle within him, and whispers that shred the minds of white-complexioned women. The constant succession of nights has driven back the sharpness of his tears, so he melted with grief, yet his eyes did not melt. Not because behind you lies a dawn for pleasures — but before you joy and mirth have died. And strange events, both odd and even — time in its acts is nothing but the father of wonders. They overpower the warriors of marked heroes and lead away knights on reed-like steeds. So I have not lost through them — I deny it not — a patience that stands in place of revealing distress. Reason does not cauterize what patience cauterizes in calamities and misfortunes. Patience is a garment and the palm's inner face is naked; reason is bare when not clothed with wealth. How wasted is reason if it does not tend its domain — and what use is a millstone turning without its axle? I became entangled in the depths of the world, and it bereaved me of my wealth and came back with honor unsoiled. How many a hardship and ease have I tasted from time, and among time's children how many a head and tail have I known. I overlook when time's turning does not overlook with its eyes toward me, and I am content when it persists in anger. And if I am tried by a severity whose roughness is hard, I smooth it so that I seem to be in play with it. I cut short the paths of grief through my body, knowing that I have not fallen short in seeking. By what swift trot of she-camels and crossing of open land does a provision reach you when it is in flight? What blame falls on me if my bowstring has not slackened in shooting, though my targets slip and I do not hit? Every day my nails are chipped as they extract poverty for me from the mine of gold. I was not like one who wanders asking the days about the Night of Power in Sha'ban or Rajab; rather, I seize the forelocks of affairs, encompassing their furthest reaches at beginning and end. I have always aimed my hopes at their targets — no vicissitude has made my seeking bad through baseness. With an estrangement like the estrangement of generosity when it flashes with a return, then betrays with disappointment and lies. When I labor for a distant goal and say I have reached it — the misfortune of being a man of letters overtakes me! And a failure that springs from an absence that has stretched in the most ominous way, rising in every place of turmoil. No one who has returned has returned without attaining his desire, and no seeker of success who has set out has been disappointed!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the misfortune of being a man of letters, the ill-luck of poets
the axle-pin of a millstone; a pivot figure
forelocks; metaphor for the beginnings/controls of affairs

context: A lengthy philosophical qasida in which Abu Tammam reflects on the vicissitudes of fortune, the misery of the poet's profession, and the virtues of patience and resolute pursuit. The poem contains a famous lament: 'The misfortune of being a man of letters,' expressing the frustration of the poet-intellectual who strives yet is denied worldly reward.

titleEn: Gloom and Weeping Were Our Mounts

verses.ajuz: وكم عدوية من سر عمر و

translationEn: We rode gloom and weeping as our mounts — yet neither these eyes nor these hearts can endure the habit of this. For indeed, a stranger when the bitterness of separation drags on finds solace in the stranger's company. And what a dwelling-place of hope — it settled there and stayed — is the pouring tear. How many an Adawite woman of the secret of Amr, whose lineage when traced is noble, has a chaste mother from Tayy, of a distinguished clan, and a noble father, hoped that a beloved would return to her — she hoped in vain; and where is a beloved for her? Had she truly seen him, she would have seen one eager, adorned by time's water with pallor — like a sword-blade stripped of its sheath, its striking-edge notched by misfortunes — a guarantor of wealth, or a hero of weeping over a land of barren fields and numerous offspring, who has reached a place where there is no water for the thirsty, no wealth to take refuge in — stripped bare, in Egypt. And what purpose is there in Egypt, now that death has taken away its eldest?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: death (personified as a separator of clans)

context: A fragment of an elegy (ritha) lamenting the death of a noble figure in Egypt, interwoven with themes of exile, the suffering of a waiting woman, and the poet's own estrangement. The poem is preserved without its opening verses.

titleEn: Days Sought Him as He Sought Them

verses.ajuz: أو راح من سلب الزمان سليبا
أن شام من حكم الزمان عجيبا

translationEn: Days sought him, and he, the seeker of their like, sought others in return, so he became at once the sought and the seeker. It is a resolution fit for a sword, yet it has been made a reed-stalk for the ropes of time. Time's calamities wooed from him a condition that bore as its offspring trials and disasters. Time's ropes cut from him a decisive cut that left misfortunes palpitating. Many a time the blow of an event grieved him, reopening scars on the inner face of his two sides. Not that the means of wealth failed him, or that he went home stripped by time's plundering; rather, it is a wonder — and no wonder — for one who has glimpsed in the decree of time a marvel: that one day his dwelling is at the point where the east ends, and another day he dwells at the west as a stranger. May hopes whose fulfillment is guaranteed by a generosity that shows you only a frowning, scowling face not exist!
context: A meditation on the reversal of fortune, praising a figure who has endured time's trials with steadfastness. The poem plays on the paradox of one simultaneously seeking and being sought, and ends with a bitter irony about generosity that only frowns.

titleEn: When I Saw the Matter Was Serious
translationEn: When I saw the matter was truly serious and found no escape from rising to it, I donned the skin of a leopard in pride and the skin of a lion split clean through. I gathered the Arab assembly, the mightiest of them, a gathering that fells the most powerful of oppressors. It demolishes the pillars of mountains in utter demolition — Tamim was a slave to our father; lions whose vigor of attack was curly-haired, and we were an army for the Prophet. On the day of Buzakhat they came to the watering place — and I compare it to Badr and compare it to Uhud. And Tayy clothed me in a mantle of glory, so that I boasted and I routed the slave.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: skin of a leopard; courage and ferocity
a mantle, a robe of honor
a place name, site of a famous battle in the Ridda wars

context: A boasting (fakhr) poem in rajaz meter where Abu Tammam celebrates his tribal lineage from Tayy and their heroic deeds, comparing the battle of Buzakhat to the Prophet's battles of Badr and Uhud in terms of importance.

titleEn: She Appeared as the Rope of Separation Tightened

verses.ajuz: وقد سهل التوديع ما وعر الهجر
خليء وما يخلو له من جوى صدر
إذا الشمس لم تغرب فلا طلع البدر
على الصدر إلا أن صائغها الشفر

verses.sadr: وما الدمع ثان عزمتي ولو أنها
verses.ajuz: سقى خدها من كل عين لها نهر
بحزم له في كل مظلمة فجر

verses.sadr: وصارعت عن مصر رجائي ولم يكن
verses.ajuz: ليصرع عزمي غير ما صرعت مصر
من الهم لم يفرغ على زبره قطر
فتى وافر الأخلاق ليس له وفر
على متنها والبر من آله بحر

verses.sadr: وما الققر بالبيد القواء بل التي
verses.ajuz: نبت بي وفيها ساكنوها هي القفر !
فأحج به أن ينجلي ولها القمر

verses.sadr: فإن كان ذنبي أن أحسن مطلبي
verses.ajuz: أساء ففي سوء القضاء لي الغدر

verses.sadr: قضاء الذي ما زال في يده الغنى
verses.ajuz: ثنى غرب آمالي وفي يدي الفقر

verses.sadr: رضيت وهل أرضى إذا كان مسخطي
verses.ajuz: من الأمر ما فيه رضا من له الأمر !

verses.sadr: أبى لي بحر الغوث أن أرأم التي
وهل خاب من جذماه في ضنء طيىء

verses.ajuz: إذا نجمت ذلت لها الأنجم الزهر

verses.sadr: لنا جوهر لو خالط الأرض أصبحت

verses.ajuz: صغت أذن للمجد ليس بها وقر
مدى اللين الا أن أعراضنا صخر
ولا نسب يدنيه منا ولا صهر

verses.sadr: اذا زينة الدنيا من المال أعرضت
وكور اليتامى في السنين فمن نبا
verses.ajuz: بفرخ له وكر فنحن له وكر

verses.sadr: أبى قدرنا في الجود إلا نباهة

verses.ajuz: عوان لهذا الناس وهو لنا بكر

verses.sadr: جرى حاتم في حلبة منه لو جرى
verses.ajuz: بها القطر شأوا قيل أيهما القطر !

verses.sadr: فتى ذخر الدنيا أناس فلم يزل
verses.ajuz: لها باذلا فانظر لمن بقي الذخر !

verses.sadr: فمن شاء فليفخر بما شاء من ندى

verses.ajuz: اذا نطقوا في مشهد خرس الدهر

verses.sadr: على كل طرف يحسر الطرف سابح
طوى بطنها الإسآد حتى لو أنه
verses.ajuz: بدا لك ما شككت في أنه ظهر
بني أسد إن كان ينفعك الخبر
لنا خطوة في أرضها ولهم فتر

verses.sadr: مساع يضل الشعر في كنه وصفها

translationEn: She appeared as the rope of separation was tightly twisted, and the farewell had been made easy by what the hardship of parting had made rough. She wept over him with the tears that the days of her breast had made him weep — one who is empty yet whose breast is never empty of burning passion. She said, 'Will you forget the full moon?' I said in feigned composure, 'When the sun does not set, the full moon does not rise.' She then displayed pearls of tears strung on her breast, but their jeweler was her eyelashes. Tears do not shake my resolve, even if each of her eyes were a river watering her cheek. I gathered the rays of my judgment and branded it with a resoluteness that has a dawn in every darkness. I wrestled my hopes away from Egypt — and nothing could have thrown my resolve except what Egypt threw. I leveled a dam like Gog's barrier before it, from grief — rain did not pour over its heights. Mounted on a swift camel that had lost the fullness of its flesh, I am a youth of perfect character but without wealth. How many a desolate wilderness I have loved, traversing its back, while the land from its kin was a sea. The true desert is not the empty wasteland; rather, it is the place that sent me away — and its inhabitants make it the desert! Whoever gambles with the days for their fruits, it is all the more fitting that the moon shine clear for them. If my fault is that I excel in my seeking, and that is an offense, then in the ill decree the betrayal falls on me. The decree of One in whose hand wealth has always been turned my hopes' sharpness while poverty was in my hand. I was resigned — but how can I be resigned when what provokes me is the very thing that contains the satisfaction of the One who commands? So I made my days bitter with a patience whose outcomes were sweet — and patience is true to its name, for it is bitter. The sea of rescue refused to let me suckle from what I have cursed, and lineage resembles lineage. Has anyone who traced his descent to the noblemen of Tayy, to Adiyy the two Adiyys, al-Qalmas or Amr, ever been disappointed? We have brilliant distinguished marks of Zaydi and Addid descent — when they rise like stars, the brightest stars bow to them. We have an essence that, had it mingled with the earth, its plains and heights would have turned to gold. Jadila and al-Ghawth, to whom the ear of glory has leaned — an ear with no deafness. Our stations are endowed with forbearance and wisdom; our youths are sages and our white-haired are learned scholars. We have softened hands with giving until they exceeded the limit of softness, yet our honor is rock. It is as if our gifts are kin to whoever comes, though no lineage or marriage brings him near to us. When the world's adornment of wealth turns away, what is more beautiful to us than praise and gratitude. He shelters the orphans in years of drought — whoever has a nestling with a nest, we are a nest for him. Our worth in generosity refuses anything but renown, so money has no worth to us ever. Let whoever wishes compete in generosity — for others it is a tame thing, but for us it is virgin. Hatim ran in its arena — had rain run in it a single stretch, one would ask: which of the two is the rain? A youth for whom people stored the world's wealth, yet he never ceased to lavish it — see then for whom the treasure remains! Whoever wishes may boast of whatever bounty he pleases, for no living tribe other than us has that boast. We gathered glory through generosity after it had dispersed, gathering it to us as the month gathers the days. By our valor, the clouds of death dropped their burden upon Najd — dark and turbid clouds — with every armored warrior whose chest is the target of lances, when entrails are ablaze and the liver swells. Escorted by the sons of death to battle, they are escorted by patience, escorted by victory. Warriors while other warriors linger at the battlefield, their lances red and their faces yellow. With cavalry in which Zayd al-Khayl has horsemen — when they speak at a great assembly time falls silent. On every swift horse that dims the eye, a swimmer — and a swimming mare whose swimming is a gallop. Night-journeying has folded her belly so that if it appeared to you, you would not doubt it was her back. A Dhabba mare that never reminds herself of what lies behind as long as a target lies ahead. Though enemies curse the evil of her morning's onset, neither wolf nor vulture can repay her gratitude. Through her, Qays ibn Aylan and al-Fazr came to know their true worth after ignorance of it. Taghlib met in her a conqueror of every conqueror, and Bakr found in our war a full-grown, first-time combatant. You know well how our lions have left the Banu Asad — if the news is of any use to you. Our unfair partition in Najd and its people: for us a step in their land and for them a span. Deeds that poetry loses itself in trying to describe fully — and yet poetry finds its way only to the least of them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: tightly twisted, said of a rope
Hatim al-Tayy, the legendary pre-Islamic figure of generosity
our valor, our courage in battle

context: A grand qasida in al-tawil meter that opens with a nasib (love prelude) lamenting departure, transitions through the poet's personal hardships and tribal pride, and culminates in an extended madih (praise) of his tribe Tayy and their legendary generosity exceeding even Hatim al-Tayy.

titleAr: ألا صنع البين الذي هو صانع
titleEn: Separation Did What Separation Does

verses.sadr: ألا صنع البين الذي هو صانع
verses.ajuz: فإن تك مجزاعا فما البين جازع

verses.sadr: هو الربيع من اسماء والعام رابع
verses.ajuz: له بلوى خبت فهل أنت رابع

verses.sadr: ألا إن صبري من عزائي بلاقع

verses.ajuz: إلى الغيث حتى جادها وهو هامع

verses.sadr: لئن كان أمسى شمل وحشيك جامعا
verses.ajuz: لقد كان لي شمل بأنسك جامع

verses.sadr: أسيء على الدهر الثناء فقد قضى
أبو منزل الهم الذي لو بغى القرى
verses.ajuz: لدى حاتم لم يقره وهو طائع

verses.sadr: له همم ما إن تزال سيوفها
ألا إن نفس الشعر ماتت وإن يكن
ترقت مناه طود عز لو ارتقت
verses.ajuz: به الريح فترا لانثنت وهي ظالع

verses.sadr: أنا ابن الذين استرضع الجود فيهم
سما بي أوس في السماء وحاتم
فأي يد في المحل مدت فلم يكن

verses.ajuz: لأيقنت أن الرزق في الأرض واسع

verses.sadr: رياح كريح العنبر الغض في الندى

verses.ajuz: كشفت قناع الشعر عن حر وجهه

verses.sadr: فيدنو إليها ذو الحجى وهو شاسع

translationEn: Behold, separation has done what separation does — if you are one who grieves, separation itself does not grieve. He is the spring to Asma, and this is the fourth year of his trial in the burnt wasteland — are you then a fourth? Indeed, my patience from my resolves is a barren wasteland, on the evening when the empty abodes stirred my longing. As if the white clouds had concealed beneath them a beloved, so their tears never cease. Hills whose meadows the east wind interceded for before the rain until it poured down on them, pouring in torrents. The face of the forenoon became for them a scene of smiling, and the damp flank of night became for them a bed. It clothed you in blossoms — a pure yellow, a brilliant white, a radiant red. Even if tonight the community of your wild creatures is united, there was a time when the community of your companions was united around me. I speak ill of time in praise, for it has judged against me with the injustice of its continuous turning. Does it crush us like the crushing of date-pits while it is solid, and devour us like locusts' devouring while it is famished? Whenever time lodges its load in my dwelling, I startle it in its flock as it grazes. Father of the dwelling of grief — one whom Hatim himself, were he to seek hospitality, would not have entertained even while willing. When the nights set upon him with disaster they tear themselves apart on him while he is armored with patience. When a calamity charges him one day, it meets its own points while he is armored with patience. He has resolves whose swords never cease to be keen-edged if only they had things to cut. Behold, the very soul of poetry has died — and if death's doom has not yet reached it, it is in its death-throes. I shall mourn the rhymes with rhymes, for they rightfully lament over it. The guardian of the shades of manliness is abandoned, and the preserver of the days of generosity is lost! A howler howled, yet glory stands between me and him with a barrier before me and a buttressing pillar. His ambitions climbed the mountain of might — had the wind climbed it even a hand's span it would have turned back limping. I am the son of those among whom generosity was suckled and named, both as a youth and as a mature man. Aws in the sky has raised me high, as have Hatim, Zayd al-Qana, the two Athrams, and Rafi. And Iyas was what Iyas was, and Arif and Haritha — the most loyal of all creatures and in their deeds. Stars that rise and mountains that tower, rains that pour and floods that surge. They passed away, yet noble deeds were to them like religious rites, for all they bequeathed them. What hand stretched out in time of drought that was not a bountiful south wind, with floods that have no resting place? They entrusted beneficence as a well-guarded deposit — what belongs to us has been squandered, yet the deposits are never lost with us. Glorious lords — had you seen the excess from their open hands, you would have been certain that provision on earth is abundant. When the spirits of their generosity flutter with giving, bounty drives them on, and aspirations inhale them. Winds like the wind of fresh ambergris in the dew, yet on the day of meeting they are howling gales. Behind them in earnest is a determined wrestling; they rose with fertile south winds as if they were south winds and north winds with no resting place. I have unveiled poetry's veil from the noble face of her visage and startled her from her nest while she was perched. With a glory that whoever hears it sees it — and a wise man draws near to it even from far away, wishing fervently that the members of his body were ears yearning to hear it when it is recited.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: young locusts that cannot yet fly
glorious lords, noble and generous men

context: A grand multi-section qasida opening with a nasib on the abandoned dwelling, moving to reflection on time and patience, then lamenting the death of poetry itself, and culminating in a genealogical madih of the poet's tribal ancestors from Tayy — Hatim, Aws, and others — praising their legendary generosity and valor.

titleEn: Tomorrow You Shall Die and Be Buried

verses.ajuz: ولا حول محتال ولا وجه مذهب
لقد قدر الأرزاق من ليس عادلا

verses.sadr: عن العدل بين الخلق فيما يقدر
verses.ajuz: فلا تأمن الدنيا وإن هي أقبلت
فما نم فيها الصفو يوما لأهله
وما لاح نجم لا ولا ذر شارق

verses.sadr: على الخلق إلا حبل عمرك يقصر

verses.ajuz: وشمر فقد أبدى لك الموت وجهه

verses.sadr: فيظهر عنه الطرف ما كان يستر
verses.ajuz: تذكر وفكر في الذي أنت صائر

verses.sadr: إليه غذا إن كنت ممن يفكر
verses.ajuz: فلا بد يوما أن تصير لحفرة

translationEn: And tomorrow in it you shall die and be buried — yet you fertilize hopes and await their offspring! Your lifespan is shorter than what you hope for. You circle around attaining what has already been provided for you, advancing toward it with hopes and retreating. This very morning's light announces your death to you, and its night announces your death too if you have any awareness. Your provision will not escape you — it is either hastened to come on a given day, or it is deferred — and there is no scheme for the schemer, no way out, and no measure to convey it but the One who decrees. Indeed, One who is not just between creatures has apportioned provisions as He decrees. Do not trust the world even if it turns toward you, for it has always betrayed and deceived. Serenity has never slept in it for its people, nor ease, except for as long as it takes to change. No star has appeared, nor has a sun risen over creatures, without the rope of your life shortening. Purify yourself and attach repentance to your sin today — perhaps if you purify yourself you shall be purified of it. Gird yourself, for death has shown you its face, and none attains salvation but one who girds himself. These nights herald your decay — they come in the evening and the days likewise come at dawn. Be sincere to the religion of God in your heart and your intention, for what you conceal will one day be revealed. A person may conceal his deed with words, but the eye reveals what was hidden. Remember, and reflect upon what you are heading toward tomorrow, if you are one who reflects — for inevitably one day you shall end up in a pit, folded within it until the Day of Resurrection.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to fertilize, to impregnate; metaphor for nurturing hopes
one who girds himself, the resolute and prepared
you are resurrected, raised up (Day of Judgment)

context: A zuhdiyya (ascetic poem) — a fragment whose opening verses are missing — meditating on the shortness of life, the inevitability of death, the unreliability of worldly provision, and the necessity of repentance and sincere devotion to God before it is too late.

titleAr: أرى ألفات قد خططن على راسي
titleEn: I See White Lines Drawn Upon My Head

verses.sadr: أرى ألفات قد خططن على راسي
وقد أمس من وصل الكواعب آيسا

translationEn: I see alif-letters drawn upon my head by the pens of white hair on parchment pages. If you ask me who draws these letters — it is the hand of the nights, drawing ink from my very breaths. In the hearts of the young women a shuddering has run for my white hair, after softness and intimacy. And I have come now to despair of union with young maidens — for the last of all hopes leads to despair.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the letter alif (ا); white hair strands resembling the letter
a shudder, a shiver of aversion
young women who are self-sufficient in beauty, charming maidens
young women with rounded breasts, maidens

context: A brief meditative poem on aging, in which Abu Tammam uses the elegant conceit that the strands of white hair on his head are like alif-letters written by the pen of time, drawing ink from his own life-breaths. The poem concludes with resigned acceptance of old age's estrangement from youth and love.

titleAr: ألم يأن تركي لا علي ولا ليا
titleEn: Is It Not Time I Left Frivolity Behind

verses.sadr: ألم يأن تركي لا علي ولا ليا
verses.ajuz: وعزمي على ما فيه اصلاح حاليا

verses.sadr: وقد نال مني الشيب وابيض مفرقي

verses.ajuz: وآل ثمود بعد عاد بن عاديا

verses.sadr: فيا ليتني من بعد موتي ومبعثى
verses.ajuz: أكون رفاتا لا علي ولا ليا

verses.sadr: لما ساغ لي عذب من الماء بارد
verses.ajuz: ولا طاب لي عيش ولا زلت باكيا

verses.sadr: على إثر ما قد كان مني صبابة

verses.ajuz: وإن كنت لم أشرك بذي العرش ثانيا

translationEn: Is it not time to abandon what is neither to my benefit nor to my advantage, and to resolve upon what will reform my condition? White hair has taken hold of me, my parting has turned white, and a greyness in the back of my head has consumed my darkness. Conditions have changed me from what I used to know them — the nights in their first youth — for the nights are not as they were. I call out to the world and it does not answer me; I try to remain — yet how can I remain? The days never cease to trim my lifespan, beyond reckoning, not like my own counting. They erase my traces, wear out my newness, and at their earliest opportunity vacate my dwelling of my place — as they did before me to Tasm and Jurhum, and the house of Thamud after the clan of Ad son of Ad. I shall be left prostrate among my family as a funeral bier, and the heirs will take possession of my pure wealth. I say to my soul when it inclines with its craving toward thoughts that have given birth to desires: are not the nights robbers of my soul's blood, as they robbed the past generations before me? And will they not lodge me in a niche at the edge of a pit in which the stretch of further nights prolongs my dwelling — as they lodged Ham, Shem, and Japheth, and Noah, and whoever took up abode in Mecca? I have thus grown familiar with death, because I have seen the fates snatching away my life. Would that after my death and resurrection I could be mere dust — neither burdening another nor burdening myself! I fear my God, then I hope in His bounty — but my fear overpowers my hope. Were it not for my hope and my reliance upon the One who has singled me out alone with His grace, both as a mature man and as a youth, not even sweet cold water would have been pleasant to me, no life would have suited me, and I would not have ceased weeping. I store up piety to the utmost of my capacity, and in my guidance I ride against the current of my own desire — following upon what was once in me of passion, on nights when I was disobedient to God. For I am worthy of fearing and being mindful of God — even though I never associated any partner with the Lord of the Throne.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the parting of the hair, the crown of the head
the back of the head, the nape
extinct ancient Arab tribes, examples of vanished civilizations
to snatch away, to cut off (life)

context: A lengthy zuhdiyya (ascetic poem) in which Abu Tammam reflects on old age, the transience of life exemplified by vanished ancient peoples, the fear of death and judgment, and his sincere turn toward God. The poem ends with a declaration of monotheism and readiness for accountability.

titleEn: Separation Fed Me Colocynth's Bitter Brew

verses.sadr: ما حسرتي أن كدت أقضي إنما
نقل فؤدك حيث شئت من الهوى
كم منزل في الأرض يألفه الفتى

translationEn: Separation made me gulp down the brew of colocynth, and separation bereaved me even though I have not truly lost a child. My regret is not that I nearly died — my soul's regret is rather that I did not. Transfer your heart wherever you wish in love — love is only for the first beloved. How many a dwelling on earth does a young man grow fond of, yet his yearning is forever for the first dwelling.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the soaked brew of colocynth, an intensely bitter medicinal plant
bereaved me of a child, caused me to grieve a loss
becomes attached to, grows fond of

context: A celebrated short ghazal containing some of Abu Tammam's most quoted verses. The third and fourth lines became proverbs: love is only truly felt for the first, and the heart's truest longing is always for its first home.

titleEn: A Visitor Who Stirred a Phantom
translationEn: A visitor visited me and stirred a phantom — were it not for him I would be in the worst state of all people. I delighted in a gazelle — though God forbid that that figure should truly be a gazelle! How can I hope for a meeting with one who dwells in Baghdad while I am in Egypt — truly I have hoped in vain! My desires presented him to my eyes, my thoughts, and my heart until I accepted the impossible. I see myself never ceasing to be the target of a nocturnal phantom — or until my body becomes a phantom itself!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a phantom, a night vision of the beloved
a gazelle; metaphor for a beautiful beloved
a nightly visitor, one who comes by night

context: A light ghazal playing on the motif of the nocturnal phantom (khayāl) of the beloved. Abu Tammam laments being in Egypt while his beloved is in Baghdad — a detail that situates the poem in his years of sojourn in Egypt.

titleEn: I Am Jealous of My Own Kiss
translationEn: I am jealous of you even on account of my own kiss, even if you grant me my wish. And I fear to see your cheek as a fixed target for the falling of gazes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I am jealous, I feel possessive jealousy
a fixed target, something set up

context: A tiny but exquisite two-verse ghazal expressing extreme jealousy: the poet is jealous even of his own kisses and dreads that others' eyes will rest upon the beloved's cheek.

titleEn: Seeking My Death Through His Rejection
translationEn: He seeks my death through his rejection, while his face — unrivaled in beauty — is my sole occupation. His glances move swiftly among all people toward what he desires, as swift as an arrow.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unique in beauty, unrivaled in handsomeness

context: A concise two-verse ghazal in which the beloved is depicted as a master of swift, lethal glances — his very rejection is a pursuit of the lover's death, and his gaze moves like an arrow.

titleEn: Handsome Yet Unjust to His Lover
translationEn: Well-proportioned, yet his justice was not well-proportioned toward a lover whose infatuation with him has long endured. Is his charm the more beautiful, or his elegance? Is his face the more beautiful, or his mind? Look — whatever beauty you have seen in anyone else, all of it belongs to him alone. If Beauty itself were asked to make a wish, it would wish to be like him. What qualities has my master gathered — had only his delay not clouded their serenity!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: delay, procrastination in fulfilling a promise

context: A playful ghazal cataloging the beloved's virtues with rhetorical questions, culminating in the witty hyperbole that even Beauty itself would wish to resemble him — marred only by his one flaw: keeping his lover waiting.

titleEn: How My Heart Was Humbled
translationEn: Misery to my heart — how it has been humbled, becoming a dwelling for sickness! I did not fear what has come to pass, and I was once carefree. I have wasted away until I see no shadow of myself in the mirror of the sun. May God pardon the one who wrongs me for what he has deemed lawful!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I wasted away, I melted from grief
the mirror of the sun, the bright face of sunlight

context: A short lyric of extreme wasting away from love, written in a truncated meter that enacts the fragmentation of the lover's self. The final verse is a prayer of forgiveness for the beloved who has wronged him.

titleEn: O Sickness of the Eyelid from My Beloved
translationEn: O sickness of the eyelid from my beloved — it has clothed me in the robe of illness! How many a heart-stricken lover have your two glances wrongfully killed! O you on whose account I am overcome by passion — you have drawn my doom close to my soul. You have drunk your fill of my blood — enough of your well-aimed arrows and darts!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the languor of the eyelid, heavy-lidded eyes of the beloved
the robe of illness; metaphor for love-sickness
one driven mad by love, one whose heart has been stolen
you were quenched, you drank your fill

context: A passionate short ghazal personifying the beloved's heavy-lidded glance as the source of the poet's illness. The arrows and blood imagery culminates in a plea to stop, as the beloved has already drunk enough of the poet's blood.

titleEn: My Eye Translates What My Tongue Cannot Say

verses.ajuz: فيظهر من وجدي الذي كنت أكتم

translationEn: My eye translates on behalf of my tongue in secret, revealing the passion I have been concealing. Is it not strange that one house contains both me and you, yet we are never alone and never speak? Lips gesture, eyebrows signal, glances are cast and a gaze offers greeting — while our tongues are prevented from expressing what we desire, and our eyes answer for us and understand!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: translates, interprets, speaks on behalf of
a signal with the eyebrow, a wink
casting sidelong glances, broken looks of longing

context: A refined ghazal on the theme of concealed love in a shared social space where lovers cannot speak openly. The eye becomes the eloquent translator of what the tongue is forbidden to say, communicating through gestures and glances.

titleAr: كيف بعدي لا ذقتم البين أنتم
titleEn: How Have You Fared Since My Departure

verses.sadr: كيف بعدي لا ذقتم البين أنتم
يا منى النفس إن قلبي وإن با
verses.ajuz: ن بي البين عندكم حيث كنتم

translationEn: How have you fared after me — may you never taste separation — tell me, since I parted from you and you parted from me! Are you as I knew you, or have the misfortunes of treacherous time changed you so that you have been faithless? O you who are my soul's desires — my heart, though separation parted me from you, is with you wherever you may be.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the treacherous, the deceitful (epithet of time)
the desires of the soul, the beloved

context: A short ghazal of parting in which the poet addresses distant companions or a beloved, asserting that though his body is separated, his heart remains with them. The treachery of time is invoked as the agent of separation.

titleAr: سلام على من لا يرد سلامي
titleEn: Greetings to One Who Does Not Return Them

verses.sadr: سلام على من لا يرد سلامي

translationEn: Greetings to one who does not return my greeting and who does not see me as worthy of conversation. And what would it cost him to answer one who greets him? It is not as though responding with a greeting would settle any obligation he owes me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a fitting place for speech, worthy of being spoken to
an obligation, a responsibility, a bond of honor

context: A compact two-verse ghazal expressing the bitterest form of the beloved's indifference: he will not even return a greeting. The second verse is a restrained reproach — even a mere salutation is refused, though it would cost nothing.

titleEn: Given the Finest Breath of Beauty

verses.sadr: من كان لم ير شمسا من سنا بشر

translationEn: You were given the most radiant of beauty's fragrances, and you surpassed the most fragrant of sweet scents. Beauty has been cast aside and perfume put to shame, and the houris of paradise have become, after God, your clients. Whoever has never seen a sun made of human radiance — we have seen it in Ali.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the most radiant, the most brilliant of them
the most fragrant, the most intense of them
the houris, the beautiful maidens of paradise

context: A short madih for a patron named Ali, employing hyperbolic praise: his beauty surpasses all earthly beauty and even that of the paradise houris, and seeing him is like seeing the sun made of human splendor.

titleEn: On Honor, Shame, and Character

verses.sadr: إذا ما رأس أهل البيت ولى
فلا والله ما في العيش خير

translationEn: If you emulate a base person in character, then you and the one you emulate are equal. I have seen that the free man avoids disgraceful acts, and faithfulness guards him from treachery. There is no hardship without an ease that will come after its severity. I have tested this time until trials and toil have benefited me. When the head of a household departs, people begin to show coldness toward them. A man lives well for as long as he feels shame, and a branch endures as long as its bark remains. By God, there is no good in life nor in the world when shame is gone. If you fear not the consequences of the nights and feel no shame, then do as you please. A base-acting man from a noble people will forever hear the howling of censure from among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you emulated, you ran alongside in character

context: A wisdom poem (hikam) in al-wafir meter containing famous proverbial verses on character, honor, and shame. The verse 'A man lives well as long as he feels shame, and a branch endures as long as its bark remains' became one of the most quoted sayings in Arabic literary tradition.

titleEn: A Scathing Invective Against a Rival
translationEn: O Utayb, son of a vile and filthy woman — have you felt safe from my arrogance and my fury? I swear a solemn oath that you deserve your fate and the disgrace that is rightfully yours. Is your claim among the tribe of Kalb a broader scandal and more particular, or is your claim among the poets? What wonder — a hunter of invective through his own honor, yet his sanctuary is forever exposed! His poetry is no match for mine — let him die of rage — and this low creature is not my peer. How could he escape my talons in a land where my earth and my sky are spread wide, with the elders of Kahlan and the strongholds of Himyar like a flood both before me and behind? These are my paternal uncles who wrapped themselves in noble deeds — and these are my fathers!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: foul-smelling, vile; an insult to the opponent's mother
talons, claws; metaphor for poetic attack
one of the two main branches of the Yemeni Arab tribes
ancient Yemeni tribe of great prestige
wrapped themselves (in turbans of nobility); achieved honor

context: A fierce hija (invective) against a poet named Utayb, combining personal insults with tribal boasting. Abu Tammam asserts the superiority of his poetry and the nobility of his lineage through the great Yemeni tribal confederations.

titleEn: I Was Told Utba Is the Poet of the Rabble

verses.ajuz: قد ضج من عودي ومن إبدائي

verses.sadr: ما كان جهلك تاركا لك غيه
ما الشمس أعجب حين تطلع للورى
إن كنت لست بمنته عن بذلها

translationEn: I was informed that Utba, the poet of the rabble, has been wailing from my returning and my appearing. When I was angered by verse, I satirized him and made his very nature the satire of my satire. Your ignorance was never going to leave you your aberration, even if you were to become the hen of al-Raqa. My forbearance toward the forbearing is unclouded, but death lies in my recklessness toward the reckless. How weak is one whose entire affair has become morning and evening a following of the worm among poets! I am amazed at people who have the forms of men yet have the private parts of women! God knows it is truly a calamity that has descended — especially upon poets. The sun rising in the west is no more astonishing than a poet who sells himself. If you are not going to stop offering yourself, then I am more entitled to it than the strangers!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the rabble, the riffraff, the common mob
the hen of al-Raqa; a proverbial insult for a base person
the hairy caterpillar; used as an insult for a vile poet
offering it, giving it cheaply; selling oneself

context: A continuation of Abu Tammam's satirical attacks on a rival poet named Utba (or Utayb), full of obscene insults typical of the hija genre. The poem escalates to accusations of effeminacy and moral degradation.

titleEn: Tell Abdun Where Has His Shame Gone
translationEn: Tell Abdun: where has that shame of yours gone? The disease of licentiousness is an incurable disease! For long you were in my estimation impregnable and guarded, as a robe is carefully preserved. Then you pushed me away without cause — so I am now equal to al-Mubaraki in your eyes. My sincere advisers told me — and it is a true saying — that to criticize one who was obscure is to praise him. They spoke truth: in invective there is an elevation for rabble-like peoples — so I have no satire for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pushed me aside, turned your flank away from me
the rabble, the lowest of people

context: An invective against a figure named Abdun, notable for its restrained conclusion: Abu Tammam withholds his full satirical power, arguing that satirizing an obscure person only raises that person's profile — so the ultimate contempt is to refuse to write hija at all.

titleEn: Utba the Most Cowardly of All Creation

verses.sadr: رميت بمن لو أن الجن ترمى

verses.ajuz: إذا ما كنت أسفل منه جنبا
إذا ما كان مثلك كان كلبا

translationEn: O Utba — you who blame most cowardly of all creatures — through your ignorance you have become a target for misfortune. You have been struck by one who, were the jinn to be struck by him, humankind would plunder them as spoils. If you compete with me you will find me a boulder for your head and dust for your mouth. You will find a fire-brand with every limb of his thrashing so violently it seems to have a heart of its own. A brother of the open wastelands who has sustained and destroyed camel-loads and their riders in its vast plains. He nearly made a new east for the East, and nearly made a new west for the West. You, meanwhile, spin the pivot of a superior millstone — yet you have never seen the pivot of that high millstone. You think you gain victory in every wrestling match with an equal when you are beneath him on your side. May my qasidas be bereaved of me if a day passes and I have not fulfilled my vow against you! And then you would be what you are — for one like me, when there exists one like you, becomes a dog.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two weighty ones: humans and jinn
to compete with me, to challenge me in poetry
the pivot of a millstone; a metaphor for the center of power

context: Another fierce invective against Utba in al-wafir meter, where Abu Tammam presents himself as a desert warrior of cosmic stature while reducing his opponent to an absurd figure who only wins when already beneath his opponent. The final verse is a crushing concluding insult.

titleEn: My Poetry Pursues You Wherever You Flee

verses.sadr: يا ابن أبي عاصم ولا عاصم
لو كنت من غرة الموالي إذن
verses.ajuz: لم تنث سوءا في غرة العرب

verses.sadr: أي مناد إلى الندى وإلى الهي
إن رمت تصديق ذاك يا أعور ال
لن يهدم الناس ما بقواأ بدا
verses.ajuz: ما قد بنوه من ذلك الحسب

translationEn: My poetry will find you wherever you flee in your seeking, even if you were to ascend the heavens by a rope. O son of Abu Asim — and there is no protector — woe to you from my force and my anger! Had you been from the vanguard of the client-tribes, you would not have spoken ill of the vanguard of the Arabs. What noble man would accept the insulting of the sons of Abd al-Karim, the noble and honorable chiefs? What caller to generosity and to valor has called out to them and not been answered? What youth among them has averted his face and not been struck on the morning of battle and not struck in return? What newborn who has seen their swords bared in war has not grown grey from fear? If you wish to verify this, O one-eyed false prophet, then observe them closely and do not melt away. People will never demolish what the noble ones have built, as long as they remain in view — that lineage of theirs. These bright stars are not like one who yesterday was a foundling in poetry and in lineage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: noble chiefs, men of high standing
the one-eyed false messiah; a severe insult
the bright stars; metaphor for noble ancestors
a foundling, one of unknown or false lineage

context: A madih and invective combined in al-munsarih meter, defending the noble family of Banu Abd al-Karim against a rival's attacks. Abu Tammam uses a series of rhetorical questions to praise the family's valor and generosity before concluding with a crushing dismissal of the opponent as a false claimant to both poetry and noble lineage.
